top_gaps: Does not address campus-specific ridesharing platforms or peer-to-peer coordination mechanisms.
Does not specifically address peer-to-peer ridesharing platforms or informal coordination tools used by campus communities.
Focuses on an urban Indian context and does not address small-town, campus, or peer-to-peer ridesharing trust dynamics.
Does not address campus-specific or peer-to-peer ridesharing platforms, nor informal coordination mechanisms.
Does not examine peer-to-peer or informal ridesharing solutions that could fill identified transit gaps.
Focuses on photo-based identity cues but does not address institutional verification or campus-scoped closed-community trust mechanisms.
Does not address trust mechanisms specific to ride-sharing or campus/small-town contexts.
Focuses on accommodation rather than ridesharing, and does not address identity verification mechanisms.
Does not address campus-scoped or identity-verified platform contexts.
Aggregated findings obscure niche platform contexts such as campus-based or P2P ridesharing communities.
Descriptive secondary review without empirical data or design implications for trust mechanisms.
Focused on racial bias in accommodation; does not address ride-sharing or non-racial identity-based trust signals.
Conceptual and normative; lacks empirical grounding in specific trust or verification mechanisms.
Technology adoption focus neglects trust-building, identity verification, or safety mechanisms within the platform.
Regional and descriptive findings do not generalize to design-level trust or identity verification insights.
Focuses on labor resistance rather than ride-coordination or campus community uses of messaging apps; does not address trust formation between strangers.
Focused on public health crisis communication rather than mobility coordination or campus-scoped platform design.
Does not address ridesharing, campus mobility, or trust in peer platforms; grassroots coordination insight is in a crisis-volunteering rather than transportation context.
Examines residential community trust rather than mobility or campus-platform design; does not address stranger-to-stranger or ride-sharing trust.
Does not address coordination or community organization beyond knowledge sharing, nor grassroots transportation or campus mobility contexts.
Does not address digital platform use, WeChat, or transportation practices among international students.
Focuses on pre-arrival decision-making rather than on-campus digital practices or mobility behaviors of Chinese international students.
Does not address digital coordination, WeChat use, or transportation practices among Chinese international students.
Does not examine specific digital platforms or informal coordination tools used to facilitate mobility transitions.
Does not address campus-scoped or peer ridesharing contexts where rating fairness and identity verification intersect.
Provider-side focus leaves consumer-facing and ride-sharing trust dynamics underexplored.
Focuses on anonymity dynamics rather than identity verification or trust-building in campus-scoped platforms.

Does not address how campus scoping or institutional affiliation shapes trust or identity rather than content.

Does not examine how geo-bounded anonymous identity interacts with safety or trust in mobility contexts.
Does not address fairness asymmetries between driver and passenger rating perspectives or the effect of institutional identity verification on reputation design.
Specific to a health intervention context; does not generalize to platform-based or campus ridesharing safety design.
Does not address shared mobility contexts or how SOS features integrate with rideshare platforms.
Focused on standalone safety apps rather than integration with shared mobility or rideshare platform safety features.

Does not empirically evaluate safety outcomes or user adoption of the proposed group coordination features.
Does not focus specifically on mobility or transportation gamification, nor on platform coordination design.
Focuses on ecological footprint broadly rather than mobility or carpooling-specific gamified incentives.
Does not address mobility-specific gamification or point-system design for coordination platforms.

Focuses on commercial platform evolution rather than closed-community or campus-scoped super-app design.

Does not address closed-community or campus-specific identity and trust design within the proposed super-app model.
Focused on government service delivery rather than peer coordination, mobility, or campus community use cases.
Addresses macro smart-city governance rather than community-level trust, identity, or mobility coordination design.
Does not specifically address rideshare or carpooling contexts, nor campus-scoped platforms where algorithmic management may intersect with institutional identity systems.
